events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl was born at Iserlohn in Westphalia on Dec. 5, 1831, the son of a legal functionary who intended him to follow the same profession, though his taste for music showed itself while he was still a child.
events.excerpt: NOHL, Carl Friedrich Ludwig, a well-known writer on music and musical subjects, was born at Iserlohn in Westphalia, on Dec. 5, 1831.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl entered the University of Bonn in 1850 to pursue legal studies, though his musical interests were already drawing him away from the law.

place.name: Bonn
place.description: City in Germany, seat of the University of Bonn

events.excerpt: He was educated at the Gymnasium of Duisburg, and in 1850 entered the University of Bonn.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl entered the Prussian Civil Service as Referendarius in Berlin in 1853, while simultaneously receiving instruction in music theory from Professor Dehn.
events.excerpt: In 1853 Nohl entered the Prussian Civil Service as Referendarius, but in 1856 his health broke down, and he had to undertake a journey to France and Italy.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl returned to Berlin in 1857 after his health had necessitated a journey to France and Italy, and continued his musical studies under Professor Kiel.
events.excerpt: He returned to Berlin in 1857, and continued his musical studies under Professor Kiel.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl finally abandoned the legal profession and settled at Heidelberg in 1858, where the University subsequently conferred upon him the degree of Doctor of Philosophy.
events.excerpt: In 1858 he finally abandoned the legal profession, and settled at Heidelberg, the University of which place conferred upon him the degree of Doctor of Philosophy (1860).

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl received the degree of Doctor of Philosophy from the University of Heidelberg in 1860, following his abandonment of the legal profession.
events.excerpt: settled at Heidelberg, the University of which place conferred upon him the degree of Doctor of Philosophy (1860).

events.summary: King Ludwig II appointed Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl an Honorary Professor in the University of Munich in 1865, recognizing his growing reputation as a music writer.

people.name: King Ludwig II
people.description: King of Bavaria, patron of music and the arts

events.excerpt: In the following year he went to Munich, where in 1865 King Ludwig II. appointed him an Honorary Professor in the University.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl returned to Heidelberg in 1872, where he resided as a teacher of musical history and aesthetics until his death.
events.excerpt: In 1872 he returned to Heidelberg, where he resided as teacher of musical history and festhetics until his death, Dec. 16, 1885.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl died at Heidelberg on Dec. 16, 1885, having served as teacher of musical history and aesthetics at the university there until the end of his life.
events.excerpt: where he resided as teacher of musical history and festhetics until his death, Dec. 16, 1885.

events.summary: Wynkyn de Worde issued the first known printed collection of carols at London in 1521, the only surviving copy's end leaf being preserved in the Bodleian Library and containing the well-known Boar's-head carol.

people.name: Wynkyn de Worde
people.description: Early English printer, successor to William Caxton

events.summary: Domenico da Nola was appointed Maestro di Cappella of the Church of the Annunziata in Naples, a position he held after his birth in Nola in the first quarter of the 16th century.

people.name: Domenico da Nola
people.description: Italian Renaissance composer, also known as Don Joan Domenico del Giovane

events.excerpt: He was afterwards Maestro di Cappella of the Church of the Annunziata, Naples.

events.summary: Claudio Merulo re-edited the Villanelle of Domenico da Nola in 1567, extending the reach of that composer's work beyond its original publications between 1541 and 1564.
events.excerpt: His Villanelle were afterwards re-edited by Claudio Merulo in 1567.

events.summary: Bellini's opera Norma, with words by Romani, was produced at Milan on Dec. 26, 1831, with Donzelli, Pasta, and Grisi in the cast.
events.excerpt: NORMA. Opera in two acts ; words by Romani, music by Bellini. Produced at Milan, Dec. 26, 1831 (Donzelli, Pasta, Grisi).

events.summary: Bellini's opera Norma was produced in Paris at the Italiens on Dec. 8, 1835, bringing the acclaimed work to French audiences several years after its Milan premiere.
events.excerpt: In Paris, Italiens, Dec. 8, 1835.

events.summary: Bellini's opera Norma was produced in London in Italian at the King's Theatre on June 20, 1833, introducing English audiences to the celebrated work.
events.excerpt: In London, in Italian, King's Theatre, June 20, 1833

events.summary: Bellini's opera Norma was produced in English at Drury Lane in London on June 24, 1837, in a translation by Planche, making it accessible to English-speaking audiences.
events.excerpt: in English (PlanoW), Drury Lane, June 24, 1837.

events.summary: Barak Norman went into partnership with Nathaniel Cross in London in 1715, carrying on a joint business at the sign of the Bass Viol in St. Paul's Churchyard, as one of the foremost makers of the old English school of viol-makers.

people.description: English viol and violoncello maker, considered one of the first to manufacture violoncellos in England

people.description: English instrument maker, partner of Barak Norman

events.excerpt: In 1715 Barak Norman went into partnership with Nathaniel Cross and carried on a joint business with him at the sign of the Bass Viol in St. Paul's Churchyard.

events.summary: Thomas Norris appeared as one of the principal soprani at the Worcester Festival in 1761, early in his career as a singer.
events.excerpt: He appeared as one of the principal soprani at the Worcester Festival, 1761, and Hereford Festival, 1762

events.excerpt: He appeared as one of the principal soprani at the Worcester Festival, 1761, and Hereford Festival, 1762

events.summary: Thomas Norris was appointed organist of Christ Church Cathedral in Oxford in 1765, marking the beginning of a long association with the university city.

place.name: Oxford
place.description: City in Oxfordshire, England, home of the University of Oxford

events.excerpt: In 1765 he was appointed organist of Christ Church Cathedral, Oxford

events.summary: Thomas Norris graduated at Oxford as Mus.B. in November 1765, with his exercise consisting of two anthems performed in the Music School on Nov. 12.
events.excerpt: in November of the same year graduated at Oxford as Mus.B., his exercise (two anthems, 'The Lord is king ' and ' I will alway give thanks ') being performed in the Music School, Nov. 12

events.summary: Thomas Norris was chosen organist of St. John's College in Oxford on Dec. 15, 1765, adding this position to his appointment at Christ Church Cathedral the same year.
events.excerpt: and on Dec. 15 was chosen organist of St. John's College.

events.summary: Thomas Norris was admitted a lay clerk of Magdalen College, Oxford on Nov. 5, 1771, further cementing his long association with the university city.
events.excerpt: On Nov. 5, 1771, he was admitted a lay clerk of Magdalen College, Oxford.

events.summary: Thomas Norris sang at the Commemoration of Handel in London in 1784, where his delivery of the final recitatives in 'Israel in Egypt' and of 'Thy rebuke' and 'Behold and see' in 'Messiah' was greatly admired.
events.excerpt: He sang at the Commemoration of Handel in 1784 (where his delivery of the final recitatives in ' Israel in Egypt,' and of ' Thy rebuke,' and ' Behold and see,' in ' Messiah,' was greatly admired)

events.summary: Thomas Norris was engaged at the Birmingham Festival in 1790, but the effort proved fatal and he expired ten days afterwards.

place.name: Birmingham
place.description: City in the West Midlands, England

events.excerpt: In 1790 he was engaged at the Birmingham Festival. But the effort proved fatal ; ten days afterwards (Sept. 3, 1790), he expired at Himley Hall, near Stourbridge, the seat of Lord Dudley and Ward.

events.summary: Thomas Norris died at Himley Hall near Stourbridge on Sept. 3, 1790, having collapsed following his performance at the Birmingham Festival ten days earlier.

place.description: Country house near Stourbridge, seat of Lord Dudley and Ward

events.excerpt: ten days afterwards (Sept. 3, 1790), he expired at Himley Hall, near Stourbridge, the seat of Lord Dudley and Ward.

events.excerpt: NORDICA, Lilian, or Lillian, nie Norton, was born May 12, 1859, at Farmington, Maine, U.S.A.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica was engaged at St. Petersburg in 1881, singing roles including Philine and Amalia in 'Un Ballo', as part of her growing international career after singing in Berlin and Dantzig.

events.excerpt: she was engaged in 1881 at St. Petersburg, and sang as Philine, Amalia in ' Un Ballo,' etc.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica made her debut in 'Faust' in Paris on July 21, 1882, having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas.
events.excerpt: On July 21, 1882, she made her début in ' Faust ' and sang on Dec. 15 in 'Hamlet,' having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang in 'Hamlet' in Paris on Dec. 15, 1882, continuing her successful season in the French capital where she had studied under Gounod and Thomas.
events.excerpt: she made her début in ' Faust ' and sang on Dec. 15 in 'Hamlet,' having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica made her debut at Covent Garden in London on March 12, 1887, appearing as Violetta under Mapleson after returning to the stage at Boston in 1885, with instant success on account of the purity of her style.

place.name: Covent Garden
place.description: Royal Opera House in London, England

events.excerpt: under him on March 12, 1887, she made her début at Covent Garden as Violetta—appearing later as Gilda and Margaret with instant success, on account of the purity of her style and the richness and roundness of her upper register.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang at the Philharmonic in London on March 24, 1887, during the same season as her Covent Garden debut.
events.excerpt: On March 24 she sang at the Philharmonic.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica created the part of Zelioa in Stanford's 'Veiled Prophet' at Covent Garden on July 26, 1893, adding a world premiere role to her distinguished repertory.

place.description: Royal Opera House in London, England

events.excerpt: On July 26, 1893, she created the part of Zelioa in Stanford's ' Veiled Prophet.'

events.summary: Daniel Norcome, having embraced the Romish Church and been deprived of his lay clerkship, went to Brussels where he became one of the instrumentalists in the Viceregal Chapel, with his name occurring in a list of chapel members in 1641.

events.summary: J. Woelfl published his pianoforte sonata 'Non Plus Ultra' in London in 1807, intending the work to express that mechanical difficulty could no further go.
events.excerpt: the title of a pianoforte sonata in F by Woelfl (op. 41), published in 1807 (?), and intended to express that mechanical difficulty could no further go.

events.summary: J. L. Dussek's sonata 'Plus Ultra' (op. 71) was published in London in 1808, dedicated provocatively to 'Non Plus Ultra' in direct response to Woelfl's challenge.
events.excerpt: The challenge was answered by 'Plus ultra,' the title affixed by the publishers to Dussek's sonata ' Le retour k Paris' in A flat (op. 71) on its publication in England. Its full title is ' Plus Ultra. A Sonata for the Pianoforte, composed and dedicated to Non Plus Ultra, by J. L. Dussek. Op. 71. London : Cianchetti and Sperati.' The date of publication of the second work is probably 1808.

events.excerpt: On May 21 and 22, 1878, she came to England and sang at the Crystal Palace with Gilmore's American band.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica made a successful debut under the stage name Nordica at Brescia on April 30, 1879, as Violetta in 'Traviata,' after studying singing in Milan with Sangiovanni.
events.excerpt: and on April 30, 1879, made a successful début, under the name of Nordica, at Brescia as Violetta in 'Traviata.'

events.summary: Lilian Nordica was engaged in St. Petersburg in 1881, singing as Philine and Amalia in 'Un Ballo,' establishing her reputation in one of Europe's leading operatic centres.

place.name: St. Petersburg
place.description: City in Russia, major operatic centre

events.excerpt: she was engaged in 1881 at St. Petersburg, and sang as Philine, Amalia in 'Un Ballo,' etc.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica made her debut in 'Faust' on July 21, 1882, having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas, beginning a period of great success in the French operatic repertoire.
events.excerpt: On July 21, 1882, she made her début in 'Faust' and sang on Dec. 15 in 'Hamlet,' having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang in 'Hamlet' in Paris on Dec. 15, 1882, having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas, as part of her highly successful operatic season.
events.excerpt: On July 21, 1882, she made her début in 'Faust' and sang on Dec. 15 in 'Hamlet,' having studied the leading soprano parts under Gounod and Thomas.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica reappeared on the stage at Boston in 1885, after a period of retirement following the death of her first husband in a balloon accident attempting to cross the English Channel, and subsequently made a long tour with Mapleson in America and the English provinces.
events.excerpt: In 1885 she reappeared on the stage at Boston, made a long tour with Mapleson in America and the English provinces

events.summary: Lilian Nordica made her debut at Covent Garden in London on March 12, 1887, as Violetta under Mapleson, appearing later as Gilda and Margaret with instant success on account of the purity of her style and the richness of her upper register.
events.excerpt: and under him on March 12, 1887, she made her début at Covent Garden as Violetta—appearing later as Gilda and Margaret with instant success, on account of the purity of her style and the richness and roundness of her upper register.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang at the Philharmonic in London on March 24, 1887, as part of her triumphant first season in the British capital.
events.excerpt: On March 24 she sang at the Philharmonic.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica created the part of Zelioa in Stanford's 'Veiled Prophet' in London on July 26, 1893, adding a significant new creation to her celebrated repertoire.
events.excerpt: On July 26, 1893, she created the part of Zelioa in Stanford's 'Veiled Prophet.'

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang Elsa in 'Lohengrin' at Bayreuth in 1894, marking her debut at the Wagner festival and a significant expansion of her dramatic repertoire.

events.excerpt: In 1894 she sang Elsa in 'Lohengrin' at Bayreuth.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica married a Hungarian tenor singer Zoltan Dome in America in 1896, a union she would later dissolve by divorce.
events.excerpt: In 1896 she married in America a Hungarian tenor singer Zoltan Dome, whom she afterwards divorced.

events.summary: Lilian Nordica sang again at the Handel Festival in London in 1897, continuing to demonstrate her versatility across operatic and choral repertoire.
events.excerpt: In 1897 she sang again at the Handel Festival.

events.summary: The opera Nordisa, a romantic opera in three acts with words and music by F. Corder, was produced by the Carl Rosa Company at Liverpool on Jan. 26, 1887, before transferring to London.
events.excerpt: NORDISA. Romantic opera in three acts, words and music by F. Corder. Produced by the Carl Rosa Company at Liverpool on Jan. 26, and at Drury Lane, May 4, 1887.

events.summary: The opera Nordisa, a romantic opera in three acts with words and music by F. Corder, was produced at Drury Lane in London on May 4, 1887, following its earlier premiere in Liverpool by the Carl Rosa Company.
events.excerpt: NORDISA. Romantic opera in three acts, words and music by F. Corder. Produced by the Carl Rosa Company at Liverpool on Jan. 26, and at Drury Lane, May 4, 1887.

events.summary: Richard Nordraak was born at Christiania on June 12, 1842, and would go on to become an important influence on Grieg's career through their shared commitment to Norwegian national music.

place.description: Capital of Norway, now called Oslo

events.excerpt: He was born at Christiania, June 12, 1842, and was a pupil of Kiel and Kullak

events.summary: Richard Nordraak died at Christiania on March 20, 1866, depriving his friend Grieg and his country of an enthusiastic supporter of Norwegian national music, having thrown himself with ardour into the cause of collecting and editing folk-songs.

place.name: Christiania
place.description: Capital of Norway, now called Oslo

events.excerpt: his early death on March 20, 1866, deprived his friend and his country of an enthusiastic supporter.

events.summary: Gounod's opera La Nonne Sanglante, with words by Scribe and Delavigne based on Lewis's 'Monk,' was produced at the Paris Grand Opéra on Oct. 18, 1854.
events.excerpt: NONNE SANGLANTE, LA. Opera in five acts ; words by Scribe and Delavigne, from Lewis's 'Monk' ; music by Gounod. Produced at the Paris Grand Opéra, Oct. 18, 1854.

events.summary: Anthony van Noordt was known as organist of the Nieuwe Kerk at Amsterdam in 1659, and in that year published a Tabulatur-Boeck for the organ containing French Psalm tunes and six Fantasias in Fugue-form.
events.excerpt: NOORDT or NOORTH, Anthony van, is only known as being in 1659 organist of the Nieuwe Kerk at Amsterdam. In that year there was published of him a Tabulatur-Boeck for the organ containing a number of the French Psalm tunes varied according to the verses, and six Fantasias in Fugue-form.

events.summary: Daniel Norcome was born at Windsor in 1576, the son of a lay-clerk of St. George's Chapel, and was himself a lay-clerk there before being deprived of his post after embracing Catholicism.

place.name: Windsor
place.description: Town in Berkshire, England, site of St. George's Chapel

events.excerpt: NORCOME, Daniel, son of a lay-clerk of St. George's Chapel, Windsor, and himself a lay-clerk, was born there in 1576.

events.summary: Daniel Norcome went to Brussels after being deprived of his lay clerkship at Windsor, where he became one of the instrumentalists in the Viceregal Chapel, having embraced the tenets of the Romish Church.
events.excerpt: Having embraced the tenets of the Romish Church, he was deprived of his lay clerkship and went to Brussels, where he became one of the instrumentalists in the Viceregal Chapel.

events.summary: Daniel Norcome contributed a madrigal, 'With angel's face and brightness,' to 'The Triumphs of Oriana' in London in 1601, his name appearing in a list of the Viceregal Chapel members as late as 1641.
events.excerpt: He contributed a madrigal, 'With angel's face and brightness,' to 'The Triumphs of Oriana,' 1601.

events.summary: Wynkyn de Worde issued the first known printed collection of carols in London in 1521, with the colophon recording it was 'newly imprinted at London in Flete strete at the signe of the Sonne.'

people.name: Wynkyn de Worde
people.description: Early English printer active in London

events.excerpt: 1521. Vynkyn de Worde. In this year he issued the first known printed collection of carols.

events.summary: Davies Gilbert published 'Some Ancient Christmas Carols with the tunes to which they were formerly sung in the West of England' in London in 1822, an important early collection of traditional English carol music.

people.name: Davies Gilbert
people.description: English collector and editor of Christmas carols

events.excerpt: 1822-28. Davies Gilbert. Some Ancient Christmas Carols with the tunes to which they were formerly sung in the West of England. London, 1822. 8vo.

events.summary: Wm. Sandys published 'Christmas Carols Ancient and Modern' in London in 1833, including specimens of French Provincial Carols, a landmark collection of English carol literature.
events.excerpt: 1833. Wm. Sandys. Christmas Carols Ancient and Modern . . . also specimens of French Provincial Carols, London, 1833, 8vo.

events.summary: Thos. Wright published 'Specimens of Old Christmas Carols selected from MSS. and printed books' in London in 1841 for the Percy Society, contributing to the growing scholarly interest in English carol tradition.
events.excerpt: 1841. Thos. Wright. Specimens of Old Christmas Carols selected from MSS. and printed books. Percy Society, 1841.

events.summary: Thos. Wright published 'Songs and Carols now first printed from a manuscript of the 15th century' in London in 1847 for the Percy Society, making previously unpublished medieval carol texts available to scholars.
events.excerpt: 1847. Thos. Wright. Songs and Carols now first printed from a manuscript of the 15th century. Percy Society, 1847.

events.summary: T. Helmore published 'Carols for Christmastide' in London in 1853, a collection praised as by far the best published in a popular form despite its modest pretensions.
events.excerpt: 1853. T. Helmore. Carols for Christmastide, folio.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl was appointed an Honorary Professor in the University of Munich in 1865 by King Ludwig II, marking a significant recognition of his contributions to musical literature.

people.name: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl
people.description: German writer on music and musical subjects

people.name: King Ludwig II.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl returned to Heidelberg in 1872, where he resided as teacher of musical history and aesthetics until his death, having previously served as Honorary Professor in Munich.

people.description: German writer on music and musical subjects

events.excerpt: In 1872 he returned to Heidelberg, where he resided as teacher of musical history and festhetics until his death, Dec. 16, 1885.

events.summary: Carl Friedrich Ludwig Nohl died at Heidelberg on Dec. 16, 1885, where he had long resided as a teacher of musical history and aesthetics, leaving behind many valuable works on Mozart and Beethoven that had gone through many editions.

events.summary: Thomas Norris graduated at Oxford as Mus.B. in November 1765, his exercise consisting of two anthems performed in the Music School on Nov. 12.
